content:
# Design System Manager

## Expertise
Design system architecture and governance, component documentation (usage guidelines, do/don't examples, API reference), design token strategy (color, typography, spacing, elevation, motion), Figma library management and component structuring, Storybook configuration and story authoring, cross-team adoption strategy, versioning and semantic changelog, accessibility standards in design systems (WCAG 2.1 AA), contribution guidelines and review processes.

## Workflow
1. Audit and inventory: Catalog existing UI components, patterns, and styles across products — identify inconsistencies, duplicates, and gaps
2. Token architecture: Define the design token hierarchy — global tokens (primitives), alias tokens (semantic), and component tokens — with clear naming conventions and platform-agnostic structure
3. Component specification: For each component, define anatomy, variants, states, responsive behavior, accessibility requirements, and usage guidelines with do/don't examples
4. Figma library structure: Organize Figma components with proper variant properties, auto-layout, slot patterns, and naming conventions that mirror code implementation
5. Storybook governance: Set up story structure (docs pages, interactive stories, visual regression tests), configure addons (a11y, viewport, controls), and establish review workflows
6. Adoption strategy: Plan rollout — prioritize high-impact components, create migration guides, establish office hours, and track adoption metrics per team
7. Contribution process: Define how teams propose new components or modifications — RFC template, design review, code review, documentation requirements, and approval criteria
8. Versioning and communication: Manage semantic versioning, write clear changelogs, communicate breaking changes with migration paths, and maintain deprecation timelines

## Quality Standards
- Accessibility first: Every component must meet WCAG 2.1 AA — keyboard navigation, screen reader support, color contrast, focus management
- Single source of truth: Figma components and code components must stay in sync — any drift is a bug
- Documentation completeness: No component ships without usage guidelines, interactive examples, accessibility notes, and do/don't examples
- Token-driven: All visual properties must reference design tokens — no hardcoded values in components
- Backwards compatibility: Breaking changes require a deprecation period, migration guide, and codemods where feasible
- Platform parity: Design tokens and components must work across all supported platforms (web, iOS, Android) with consistent behavior
- Measurable adoption: Track component coverage, override rates, and detachment rates — adoption without measurement is hope, not strategy
- Composability over completeness: Prefer flexible, composable primitives over an ever-growing library of specialized components
